exercises.title: Fixed Mindset Thought Log
exercises.description: For one week, keep a log of fixed-mindset thoughts as they occur. When you notice a thought like 'I'm not good at this' or 'They're just naturally talented,' write it down, then transform it into a growth-mindset reframe. Track how many fixed-mindset thoughts you catch and reframe each day.
hints: Carry a small notebook or use a notes app on your phone
Common triggers: criticism, comparison, challenges, mistakes
The goal isn't to never have fixed-mindset thoughts — it's to notice and reframe them

solution.code:
Fixed Mindset Thought Log — Week Results:

Monday:
- Fixed: 'I'm terrible at public speaking' (after a presentation)
- Reframe: 'I need more practice and better preparation strategies'
- Action: Watched a TED talk on presentation skills

Wednesday:
- Fixed: 'She's just naturally more organized than me' (comparing to colleague)
- Reframe: 'She's likely developed systems and habits I haven't learned yet'
- Action: Asked her about her organizational methods

Friday:
- Fixed: 'I'll never understand this technical concept' (studying)
- Reframe: 'I need a different learning approach — maybe a video explanation'
- Action: Found a tutorial that explained it differently; understood it in 20 minutes

Week summary:
- Total fixed-mindset thoughts caught: 12
- Successfully reframed: 10 (83%)
- Most common trigger: comparison with others
- Most effective reframe: 'I haven't practiced this yet'
- Key insight: Fixed-mindset thoughts feel true in the moment but are rarely accurate when examined

exercises.title: Process vs Person Praise Experiment
exercises.description: For two weeks, consciously change how you give praise to others (and yourself). Replace person praise ('You're so smart/talented') with process praise ('Great strategy,' 'I noticed how hard you worked on that'). Track the responses you get and any changes in behavior or attitude.
hints: Notice how it feels different to give process praise
Apply the same principle to your self-talk

solution.code:
Process Praise Experiment — Week Results:

Colleague (project completion):
Before: 'You're amazing! You're a natural at this.'
After: 'I noticed you tested three different approaches before finding the best one. That persistence paid off.'
Response: They seemed more engaged, asked questions about my approach too.

Child/nephew (homework):
Before: 'You're so smart!'
After: 'You tried a really creative approach to that problem.'
Response: They asked for a harder problem instead of stopping.

Self-talk (after completing task):
Before: 'I'm good at this'
After: 'That approach worked well — I should remember this strategy'
Effect: Felt more motivated to tackle the next challenge

Team meeting:
Before: 'Sarah is brilliant'
After: 'Sarah's research process really showed — she found three sources no one else considered'
Effect: Team started discussing processes rather than praising individuals

Key observations:
- Process praise feels more genuine and specific
- Recipients are more likely to repeat the praised behavior
- It normalizes effort and strategy as the path to success

exercises.title: The Learning Challenge
exercises.description: Choose a skill you've always wanted to learn but have avoided because 'you're not naturally good at it.' Spend 30 minutes daily for two weeks practicing it. Document: what you tried, what went wrong, what strategies you adjusted, and any improvement. At the end, compare your Day 1 performance to Day 14.
hints: Choose something with measurable progress (drawing, instrument, coding, language)
Record yourself on Day 1 and Day 14 for comparison
Focus on the PROCESS of learning, not just the outcome

solution.code:
Learning Challenge: Learning to draw portraits

Day 1: Drew a self-portrait from a mirror. Complete proportions wrong, features misaligned. Rated 2/10. Felt discouraged.

Day 3: Watched tutorials on facial proportions. Drew using the Loomis method (grid-based approach). Better proportions but still awkward. Rated 3/10.

Day 7: Started focusing on one feature at a time (eyes first, then nose). Discovered that drawing the shapes AROUND features (negative space) was easier. Rated 5/10.

Day 10: Drew a friend from a photo. Used all techniques learned. Recognizable as a person! Rated 6/10.

Day 14: Drew another self-portrait. Used grid, negative space, and feature-by-feature approach. Much improved. Rated 7/10.

Key learnings:
- Initial failure was not about 'lack of talent' — it was lack of specific techniques
- Breaking the skill into sub-skills (proportions, shading, features) made it manageable
- Recording progress made growth visible and motivating
- The biggest improvement came from changing my approach, not just 'trying harder'

Mindset shift: 'I'm not artistic' → 'I haven't learned drawing techniques yet'
